lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Here is the place, my lord; good my lord, enter:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The tyranny of the open night’s too rough

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: For nature to endure.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let me alone.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good my lord, enter here.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Wilt break my heart?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I had rather break mine own. Good my lord, enter.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou think’st ’tis much that this contentious storm

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Invades us to the skin: so ’tis to thee;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But where the greater malady is fix’d,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The lesser is scarce felt. Thou’ldst shun a bear;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But if thy flight lay toward the raging sea,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou’ldst meet the bear i’ the mouth. When the mind’s free,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The body’s delicate: the tempest in my mind

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Doth from my senses take all feeling else

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Save what beats there. Filial ingratitude!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Is it not as this mouth should tear this hand

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: For lifting food to’t? But I will punish home:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, I will weep no more. In such a night

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To shut me out! Pour on; I will endure.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: In such a night as this! O Regan, Goneril!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Your old kind father, whose frank heart gave all—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, that way madness lies; let me shun that;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No more of that.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good my lord, enter here.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Prithee, go in thyself: seek thine own ease:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: This tempest will not give me leave to ponder

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: On things would hurt me more. But I’ll go in.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To the Fool. In, boy; go first. You houseless poverty—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nay, get thee in. I’ll pray, and then I’ll sleep. Fool goes in.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Poor naked wretches, whereso’er you are,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That bide the pelting of this pitiless storm,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How shall your houseless heads and unfed sides,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Your loop’d and window’d raggedness, defend you

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: From seasons such as these? O, I have ta’en

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Too little care of this! Take physic, pomp;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Expose thyself to feel what wretches feel,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That thou mayst shake the superflux to them,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And show the heavens more just.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Fathom and half, fathom and half! Poor Tom!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Come not in here, nuncle, here’s a spirit. Help me, help me!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Give me thy hand. Who’s there?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: A spirit, a spirit: he says his name’s poor Tom.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: What art thou that dost grumble there i’ the straw? Come forth.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Away! the foul fiend follows me!

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Through the sharp hawthorn blows the cold wind.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Hum! go to thy cold bed, and warm thee.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hast thou given all to thy two daughters?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And art thou come to this?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Who gives any thing to poor Tom? whom the foul fiend hath

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: led through fire and through flame, and through ford and whirlipool e’er

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: bog and quagmire; that hath laid knives under his pillow, and halters

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: in his pew; set ratsbane by his porridge; made him proud of

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: heart, to ride on a bay trotting-horse over four-inched bridges, to course

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: his own shadow for a traitor. Bless thy five wits! Tom’s a-cold—O,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: do de, do de, do de. Bless thee from whirlwinds, star-blasting, and

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: taking! Do poor Tom some charity, whom the foul fiend vexes: there

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: could I have him now—and there—and there again, and there.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What, have his daughters brought him to this pass?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Couldst thou save nothing? Didst thou give them all?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Nay, he reserved a blanket, else we had been all shamed.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Now, all the plagues that in the pendulous air

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hang fated o’er men’s faults light on thy daughters!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: He hath no daughters, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Death, traitor! nothing could have subdued nature

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To such a lowness but his unkind daughters.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Is it the fashion, that discarded fathers

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Should have thus little mercy on their flesh?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Judicious punishment! ’twas this flesh begot

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Those pelican daughters.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Pillicock sat on Pillicock-hill: Halloo, halloo, loo, loo!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: This cold night will turn us all to fools and madmen.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Take heed o’ the foul fiend: obey thy parents; keep thy word

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: justly; swear not; commit not with man’s sworn spouse; set not thy

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: sweet heart on proud array. Tom’s a-cold.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What hast thou been?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: A serving-man, proud in heart and mind; that curled my hair; wore

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: gloves in my cap; served the lust of my mistress’ heart, and

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: did the act of darkness with her; swore as many oaths as

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I spake words, and broke them in the sweet face of heaven:

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: one that slept in the contriving of lust, and waked to do

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: it: wine loved I deeply, dice dearly: and in woman out-paramoured the

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Turk: false of heart, light of ear, bloody of hand; hog in

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: sloth, fox in stealth, wolf in greediness, dog in madness, lion in

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: prey. Let not the creaking of shoes nor the rustling of silks

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: betray thy poor heart to woman: keep thy foot out of brothels,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: thy hand out of plackets, thy pen from lenders’ books, and defy

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: the foul fiend. Still through the hawthorn blows the cold wind: Says

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: suum, mun, ha, no, nonny. Dolphin my boy, my boy, sessa! let

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: him trot by.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, thou wert better in thy grave than to answer with thy

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: uncovered body this extremity of the skies. Is man no more than

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: this? Consider him well. Thou owest the worm no silk, the beast

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: no hide, the sheep no wool, the cat no perfume. Ha! here’s

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: three on ’s are sophisticated! Thou art the thing itself: unaccommodated man

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: is no more but such a poor bare, forked animal as thou

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: art. Off, off, you lendings! come unbutton here. Tearing off his clothes.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Prithee, nuncle, be contented; ’tis a naughty night to swim in. Now

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: a little fire in a wild field were like an old lecher’s

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: heart; a small spark, all the rest on’s body cold. Look, here

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: comes a walking fire.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: This is the foul fiend Flibbertigibbet: he begins at curfew, and walks

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: till the first cock; he gives the web and the pin, squints

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: the eye, and makes the hare-lip; mildews the white wheat, and hurts

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: the poor creature of earth.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: How fares your grace?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What’s he?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who’s there? What is’t you seek?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What are you there? Your names?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Poor Tom; that eats the swimming frog, the toad, the tadpole, the

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: wall-newt and the water; that in the fury of his heart, when

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: the foul fiend rages, eats cow-dung for sallets; swallows the old rat

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: and the ditch-dog; drinks the green mantle of the standing pool; who

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: is whipped from tithing to tithing, and stock-punished, and imprisoned; who hath

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: had three suits to his back, six shirts to his body, horse

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: to ride, and weapon to wear;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What, hath your grace no better company?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: The prince of darkness is a gentleman: Modo he’s call’d, and Mahu.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Our flesh and blood is grown so vile, my lord,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That it doth hate what gets it.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Poor Tom’s a-cold.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go in with me: my duty cannot suffer

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To obey in all your daughters’ hard commands:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Though their injunction be to bar my doors,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And let this tyrannous night take hold upon you,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yet have I ventured to come seek you out,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And bring you where both fire and food is ready.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: First let me talk with this philosopher.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What is the cause of thunder?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good my lord, take his offer; go into the house.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I’ll talk a word with this same learned Theban.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What is your study?

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: How to prevent the fiend, and to kill vermin.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let me ask you one word in private.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Importune him once more to go, my lord;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: His wits begin to unsettle.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Canst thou blame him?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His daughters seek his death: ah, that good Kent!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He said it would be thus, poor banish’d man!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thou say’st the king grows mad; I’ll tell thee, friend,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am almost mad myself: I had a son,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now outlaw’d from my blood; he sought my life,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But lately, very late: I loved him, friend;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No father his son dearer: truth to tell thee,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The grief hath crazed my wits. What a night’s this!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do beseech your grace—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, cry your mercy, sir. Noble philosopher, your company.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Tom’s a-cold.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In, fellow, there, into the hovel: keep thee warm.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come let’s in all.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This way, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With him; I will keep still with my philosopher.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good my lord, soothe him; let him take the fellow.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Take him you on.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Sirrah, come on; go along with us.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come, good Athenian.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No words, no words: hush.

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: Child Rowland to the dark tower came,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: His word was still—Fie, foh, and fum,

lines.speaker: EDGAR
lines.text: I smell the blood of a British man.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Lear, Kent, and Fool.
The Fool runs out from the hovel.
Enter Edgar disguised as a mad man.
S. Withold footed thrice the old; He met the night-mare, and her nine-fold; Bid her alight, And her troth plight, And, aroint thee, witch, aroint thee!
But mice and rats, and such small deer, Have been Tom’s food for seven long year.
Beware my follower. Peace, Smulkin; peace, thou fiend!

glosses.definition: Lear fears going inside the hovel will let grief kill him
the storm — both meteorological and of the mind
worse illness — his filial grief outweighs the storm
strikes in my heart — where his greater pain lives
open, ingenuous spirit
a child's thanklessness — Lear names his wound
his own mouth feeding the hand that now strikes him
pursue the matter to the end
Lear's first great act of imaginative sympathy — for the homeless he never noticed
pitiless; pelting (as a storm)
unsheltered people
starved bodies
clothing so tattered it is full of holes and windows

glosses.phrase: O, I have ta’en / Too little care
glosses.definition: Lear's recognition of his previous indifference to poverty
swallow your medicine, royalty
the superfluous — what the rich should share with the poor
Edgar's Poor Tom opening — depth-call, as if sounding deep water in the hovel
Poor Tom — Edgar's Bedlam-beggar persona
the devil — Poor Tom's constant companion in his jargon
a nonsense rhyme-word (also slang for penis) — Poor Tom's ravings deliberately incoherent
Poor Tom's mock-catechism — twisted folk-piety
haughty — Poor Tom's past sins as imagined autobiography
a lover's token worn on the hat
Muslim; by extension, the sexually licentious of Elizabethan imagination
borrowed clothing — Lear starts to strip, seeking 'unaccommodated man'
the bare forked creature — human being stripped of culture and clothing
the human body — two legs forking from a trunk — the play's most compressed image of reduced man
pelicans were said to feed their young with their own blood; 'pelican daughters' means children who drain their parents' life — Lear's specific accusation
demon from Samuel Harsnett's 1603 'Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures' — a Jesuit-exorcist source Shakespeare read while writing Lear
the evening bell signalling fire-covering (couvre-feu)
cataracts on the eye — a folk-medicine ailment
causes squinting
Kent's persistent solicitude amid the madness
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — a minor devil-name
the devil himself
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — one of the four named devils Edgar/Tom invokes
demon from Harsnett's 1603 Declaration — another named devil; Mahu was said to have possessed one of Harsnett's case-subjects
Lear's new name for Poor Tom — a deep irony given Tom's gibberish
Lear's serious question to Poor Tom, whom he now takes as sage
Greek sage — Tiresias and Oedipus were both Theban
the philosopher Tom has become in Lear's eyes — the play's deepest irony about wisdom
Lear wants Poor Tom as his permanent philosopher
Greek — second compliment to Tom

glosses.phrase: Child Rowland to the dark tower came
glosses.definition: fragment of a Scottish ballad about the Arthurian knight Rowland — Tom's ravings include legendary scraps
fragment from Jack the Giant-Killer — Tom's disordered quotation
oppression — Kent on the storm
as if the mouth bit the hand that fed it — Lear's filial grief as anatomy
Poor Tom's mock-blessing against weather
malign astral influence causing disease
pestilence
Poor Tom's imagined suicide-method — rat poison in food
stall; raised bench
Poor Tom's description of the demon-lashed journey
Poor Tom's catalogue of food-swallowed vermin — 'eats the swimming frog, the toad...'
three changes of clothes — the worldly minimum for a gentleman

glosses.phrase: prince of darkness is a gentleman
glosses.definition: Poor Tom's mock-heraldry — the devil has manners
a nonsense rhyme — child's jingle with sexual pun
the five faculties — common sense, imagination, fantasy, estimation, memory
astral poisoning
the devil, but Tom figures him as a gentleman
made complex by civilisation — Lear contrasting clothed man with bare man

annotations.title: 'Poor naked wretches' — Lear's first act of imagination
annotations.body: Before entering the hovel, Lear pauses: 'Poor naked wretches, wheresoe'er you are, / That bide the pelting of this pitiless storm, / How shall your houseless heads and unfed sides, / Your loop'd and window'd raggedness, defend you / From seasons such as these? O, I have ta'en / Too little care of this! Take physic, pomp; / Expose thyself to feel what wretches feel, / That thou mayst shake the superflux to them, / And show the heavens more just.' This is the play's most remarkable moral moment — the king, stripped of his retinue, imagining for the first time the lives of those he ruled. 'Take physic, pomp' is the imperative he directs at his own past self: let royalty swallow the medicine of knowing what the poor endure. Critics read this as Lear's redemption-beginning, but its force comes from its too-lateness: the imagination arrives at the moment he is powerless to act on it. This is the play's argument for why moral imagination requires suffering.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'The Avoidance of Love' in Disowning Knowledge (1987)

annotations.title: Poor Tom's demons — Harsnett's 1603 Declaration as source
annotations.body: Flibbertigibbet, Smulkin, Modo, Mahu, Obidicut, Hobbididance — Poor Tom's repertoire of demon-names is not random invention but direct borrowing from Samuel Harsnett's 'A Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures' (1603). Harsnett's book was an exposure of staged exorcisms by Jesuit priests during the 1580s, naming the demons they claimed had possessed their subjects. Shakespeare, who was almost certainly reading the Declaration while writing Lear in 1605, has Edgar/Tom recite the names Harsnett's frauds had used. The effect is layered: Edgar is pretending to be possessed, using the language of actual frauds who pretended to be possessing, to fool demons who themselves are frauds. This is Shakespeare's most compact skepticism about religious spectacle — the theatrical demons of Poor Tom's madness turn out, source-wise, to be theatrical demons all the way down.
sources: Samuel Harsnett, A Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures (1603)
Stephen Greenblatt, 'Shakespeare and the Exorcists' in Shakespearean Negotiations (1988)

annotations.title: 'Unaccommodated man' — Lear strips
annotations.body: Seeing Poor Tom in rags, Lear says: 'Is man no more than this? Consider him well... Thou art the thing itself: unaccommodated man is no more but such a poor, bare, forked animal as thou art.' He begins tearing off his own clothes: 'Off, off, you lendings!' The gesture is Lear's attempt to become 'the thing itself' — a creature without the false accommodations of culture. This is the play's most radical reduction. 'Poor, bare, forked animal' is the human as evolutionary fact: two legs forking from a trunk, nothing more. Shakespeare never comes closer to a non-theological anthropology than this line. Edgar's disguise has given Lear a mirror in which he sees what man is stripped of custom — and Lear insists on joining that image.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: 'Learned Theban' — wisdom in madness
annotations.body: Lear's insistence on treating Poor Tom as a philosopher ('Theban,' 'Athenian,' 'first let me talk with this philosopher') is the play's deepest irony about knowledge. Tom's speech is a jumble of demon-names, ballad-fragments, and folkloric oaths, and Lear takes it for wisdom. But Lear is not wrong. In a world where the sane (Goneril, Regan, Edmund) are the predators and the wise (Kent, Cordelia) have been banished, Tom's deranged honesty is more trustworthy than any coherent speech in the play. Lear's philosophical promotion of Tom is comedy of an extreme kind — but it also ratifies the play's argument that the truths of the broken are the only truths left. This is 'reason in madness' (Edgar's phrase in 4.6) at its opening instance.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: What does Lear's 'Poor naked wretches' speech (lines ~28–42) argue?
options: That he wants to return to the castle
That royalty should 'take physic' — expose itself to the suffering of the poor so that wealth might be redistributed and the heavens made more just; moral imagination born of stripped power
That the poor deserve their fate

trials.prompt: Where do Poor Tom's demon-names (Flibbertigibbet, Smulkin, Modo, Mahu) come from?
options: Samuel Harsnett's 1603 'Declaration of Egregious Popish Impostures,' which exposed staged Jesuit exorcisms and named the demons the frauds had used

trials.prompt: What does 'unaccommodated man' mean in Lear's reduction speech?
options: A man without a hotel room
Man stripped of culture and custom — the 'poor, bare, forked animal' that remains when clothing, rank, and shelter are removed

trials.prompt: Why is Lear's elevation of Poor Tom to 'philosopher' ironically correct?
options: In a world where the sane are predators and the wise banished, the broken Tom's deranged honesty is more trustworthy than any coherent speech in the play — 'reason in madness'
